coredata.dc:description: Globally, antibiotic consumption surpasses thousands of tons on every year and even during this alarming state, the abundant traces of antibiotics have been found in the environment especially on water bodies and food producing creatures. The high accumulation of Chloramphenicol (CPL) may cause microbial resistance and lowering the general immunity. All these finding urges to develop a sensitive detection system for CPL. The present work describes the fabrication of Molecular Imprinted Polymer (MIP) grafted on Nitrogen and Sulfur doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N, S-CQDs) as a probe for the nanomolar level detection of CPL in food and clinical samples by using fluorescence technique. The developed N, S-CQDs based MIP sensor exhibits a bright fluorescence response and high recognition capability on CPL. The proposed sensor system has shown the detection limit of 7.65 nM and the linearity range was found between 0.24 μM and 26.1 μM. The real time consistency of the proposed sensor system has been verified by applying on clinical and food samples. Interestingly, we have received an excellent recovery varies from 97 % to 103 %. All these research outcomes further boost up the novel strategy to develop MIP based fluorescence probe for the detection of CPL.

head.ce:title: A novel molecular imprinted polymer grafted on N, S Co-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescence sensor for chloramphenicol in food and clinical samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Vandalur, Chennai 600 048, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Vandalur, Chennai 600 048, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation for the Sensing of Chloramphenicol using MST-CQDs/MIP and corresponding binding process.

Globally, antibiotic consumption surpasses thousands of tons on every year and even during this alarming state, the abundant traces of antibiotics have been found in the environment especially on water bodies and food producing creatures. The high accumulation of Chloramphenicol (CPL) may cause microbial resistance and lowering the general immunity. All these finding urges to develop a sensitive detection system for CPL. The present work describes the fabrication of Molecular Imprinted Polymer (MIP) grafted on Nitrogen and Sulfur doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N, S-CQDs) as a probe for the nanomolar level detection of CPL in food and clinical samples by using fluorescence technique. The developed N, S-CQDs based MIP sensor exhibits a bright fluorescence response and high recognition capability on CPL. The proposed sensor system has shown the detection limit of 7.65 nM and the linearity range was found between 0.24 μM and 26.1 μM. The real time consistency of the proposed sensor system has been verified by applying on clinical and food samples. Interestingly, we have received an excellent recovery varies from 97 % to 103 %. All these research outcomes further boost up the novel strategy to develop MIP based fluorescence probe for the detection of CPL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The antibiotic ingredients are always strived to inhibit or kill the microbial pathogens, however more importantly the continuous exposure of these antibiotics over the environment can enhance the gene resistance of high-risk pathogens and bacteria against the same [1]. Chloramphenicol (CPL), a frequently used chlorinated aromatic nitro antibiotic with exceptional activity against both gram negative and gram positive bacteria [2,3]. The wide applications of CPL encompasses the activity against various infections caused by microbes like fever, typhoid, meningitis and bronchitis even highly used against common cold [4,5].

Although CPL has a wide-ranging bacteriostatic action towards the prevention of peptidyl transferase activity of the bacteriological ribosome, it is not readily biocompatible and complete elimination from the environment through biological process are still a matter of concern [6]. Based on the probable side effects, CPL is placed in Group 2A chemicals. However, the lack of proper treatment and high dosage of CPL leads to myelosuppression phenomena causes drastic decrease in immunity and aplastic anaemia causes inhibition of producing the new blood carrying cells [7,8]. All of these side effects and toxicity of CPL has limited and imposed strict usage restrictions by European Union and other nations [9]. The maximum approved residue limit of CPL in food and medicinal products are set to be 0.3 µg/kg and the introduction of reliable and accurate method for the detection of CPL from environmental samples and food residues plays a vital role [10]. The prevailed methods like chromatographic especially HPLC, LC-MS, Surface Enhanced Raman spectroscopy, pressurized liquid phase extraction, Solid Phase Extraction, and microbiological assays exhibits highly sensitive detection of CPL [11,12]. On the other hand, the prolonged sample preparation time, poor selectivity, expensive instruments and the restrictions in handling are some of the underlying obstacles may restricts the continuous usage of these instruments. In contrast, the high selectivity and more recyclability of certain fluorescent probes are auspicious aspirants for the detection of trace quantities of antibiotics [13].

To add this, recently emerged Molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) are a new class of polymeric receptors received much attention from the researchers due to its wide applications including sensors, photocatalyst and drug delivery [14]. In MIPs, the polymeric chain and functional monomers are located around the targeted molecule (template) through mostly by covalent or non-covalent bonding interactions [15,16]. The use of functional monomers like APTES and TEOS with hydrophilic nature is considered as a best material which may help to disperse the MIP in aqueous solution easily. The polymeric chain formation can be completed in the presence of initiators and crosslinkers by different methods [17]. Later, consistent washing or by solvent extraction methods, the template molecules can be removed and leaving specific cavity or binding sites matching with template molecule size and shape [18].

These days, the fluorescent probe mediated MIPs are getting importance by considering the ease in application. Among the different types of fluorescent materials, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) are emerged as a unique photoluminescent material with advanced biocompatibility and water solubility on comparison with other semiconductor quantum dots [19]. The high sensitivity and ease in use makes CQDs as a promising material for sensor applications. The progressive stability, size dependent fluorescence properties, less toxicity of CQDs makes odd one out from other quantum dots [20,21]. Numerous approaches are followed by the researchers to enhance the fluorescence behaviour of CQDs. Even though the high biocompatibility and ease in application makes CQDs a way superior candidate for several applications, the deprived probe sensitivity and instability in the fluorescence emission response are the main challenges to be improved further. In order to overcome these issues, the heteroatom doping offer a hopeful approach. Nitrogen doped CQDs efficiently improved the fluorescence emission properties. Recently, S and N co-doped CQDs was synthesized via microwave method and utilized for detection of metal ions and cell imaging [22]. Another group developed co doped CQDs from L-Cysteine for the sensitive detection of fluazinam [23]. Moreover, other strategies such as surface functionalization and passivation induces more cost and environmental contaminations. Also, the presence of S and N atoms on the CQDs surface can create emission trap states for excited electrons [24]. In this work we have presented Nitrogen and Sulphur doped CQDs for the first time from 6-mercapto-s-triazolo(4,3-b)-s-tetrazine (MST). Even though CQDs are exhibiting wide range luminescence activities, the challenging concerns are the lack of selectivity or specificity of CQDs towards the target analytes due to the overlapping emission spectra of samples, the possibilities of self-quenching due to the instability of CQDs in presence of water samples and other species present in the sample plays an obstacle against the wide applications [25]. Moreover, the autofluorescence properties of target molecules may interfere and gave false results are the major drawbacks of bare CQDs. In order to improve the selectivity of CQDs, surface should be modified by molecules with selective recognition [26,27].

The present work has been established for the preparation of CQDs with precursor as MST from Purpald (4-amino-3-hydrazino-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole). Further, it was used for the preparation of MST derived N, S doped CQDs (MST-CQDs) for the first time. The MST-CQDs shows strong blue emission at 426 nm up on excitation at 330 nm. Further, MIPs was modified by reverse micro emulsion method. The fabricated MST-CQDs/MIP was used for the selective detection of CPL antibiotic in clinical and environmental samples by using this template assisted method.

The chemicals include Purpald, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %), Formaldehyde (HCHO, 98 %), 3-Aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES, 98 %), Triethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %), Triton-x-100 (99 %), Cyclohexane (99 %), n-Butanol (99 %), Aqueous ammonia (98 %), Chloramphenicol (CPL, 98 %), Sodium Chloride (NaCl, 98 %), Topiramate (TP, 97 %), Cephalexin (CX, 98 %), Ascorbic acid (AA, 97 %), Folic acid (FA, 99 %), Amlodipine (AM, 98 %), Tetracycline (TC, 97 %), Mercaptopurine (MP, 98 %), Uric acid (UA, 98 %), Omeprazole (OP, 97 %), Citric acid (CA, 98 %), Disodium orthophosphate (97 %), Monosodium diphosphate (98 %), Ethanol (99 %) were bought from Sigma Aldrich and used as received without any further purifications. The honey and milk were bought from SPAR hypermarket in Chennai, India. The lake water was collected from Madambakkam lake in Chennai, India. Double distilled water was used for the entire work.

UV–visible optical studies were achieved on a Perkin-Elmer (35 Lambda) spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Horiba FluoroMax Plus Spectrofluorometer, the images of CQDs in atomic dimension were obtained by using High resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM JEM-2100 JEOL, Japan). The presence of various functional group and nature of chemical bond is obtained by using Fourier Transform Infrared (Jasco Rapid Scan FT/IR-6600) Spectrometer, the crystallinity of the CQDs was investigated by X ray diffractometer made by Bruker. Shimadzu Axis 1165 high-performance multi-technique analysis were used to study the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) studies with energy of 80 eV. The N2 absorption or desorption analysis for the surface analysis (BET) was done by using Quantachrome Instruments, Autosorb IQ series. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was done by using a thermogravimetric analysis instrument [Perkin Elmer, Diamond] at a heating ramp of 2 °C/min.

The CQDs starting material was prepared at room temperature with Slight modification from previous report [28]. Likely, 500 mg of Purpald was dissolved in 60 mL of double distilled water (DD water) were further ultrasonicated for 10 min (min) until the powder was dispersed all over the solvent. Then the solution kept for mechanical stirring for 20 min. Further 1M of NaOH 6 mL was added drop wise until complete dissolution occurs. Thus, the entire solution become monodispersed and further stirred for 10 min. Followed by 6 mL of HCHO was added in to the mixture and continue the stirring. The colour of the mixture was gradually change to purple within 15 min indicating the formation of new derivative of Purpald specifically, MST. The large unreacted particles were removed by centrifugation. Finally, the MST solution was dried in a hot air oven at 80°C for overnight and dried powder was collected and used for the synthesis of MST- CQDs.

The prepared MST powder got weighed, 40 mg was dissolved in 20 mL of water and stirred for 15 min. The entire solution become purple in colour. After continues stirring for 15 min, the whole solution was transferred in to a 25 mL Teflon lined autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was set for 6 hrs at 160°C in a muffle furnace. After the reaction time, the autoclave was set for cool down. Then, the obtained yellow coloured solution was transferred into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 20 min in order to remove the unreacted particles. The centrifuged solution was purified by 3.5kDa dialysis tube for 6 hrs. The water was changed in every 30 min (Scheme 1 A). Further, the MST-CQDs solution was stored in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MST-CQDs grafted Molecular imprinted polymer (MST-CQDs/MIP)
ce:section.ce:para: The MST-CQDs/MIP was prepared by reverse microemulsion method with little modifications from previous report [29]. By this method, the silica based CQDs/MIP was obtained under mild condition. The commercially available Triton-x-100 act as surfactant and cyclohexane as continuous phase. Initially, 15 mL of cyclohexane mixed with 4 mL of triton -x-100 in a glass beaker followed by the addition of n-Butanol slowly. The whole mixture was magnetically stirred for 10 min until a clear solution obtained. Then, 2 mL of prepared MST-CQDs was added and continue the stirring. The strong interaction between surfactant and CQDs allows the encapsulation of mixture with in the aqueous phase of microemulsion. To this mixture, the cross linker TEOS with 200 μL was added followed by 150 μL of aqueous ammonia. Keep the stirring for next 2 hrs with mild stirring. In the final stage of the reaction, 15 mg of CPL (template) was mixed with 2.5 mL of cyclohexane and added to the stirring mixture slowly. Finally, 250 μL of APTES (monomer) was added and kept the polymerization reaction for 12 hrs in a dark room condition. After the reaction time, the microemulsion system was broken by adding acetone and stir for another 10 min. The mixture was washed by ethanol for 2 times and centrifuge the precipitate. To this solid, add methanol and acetic acid with ratio of 9:1 and until the removal of CPL template. It was confirmed by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of each washing solution. The washing could be continued until no template molecules found in UV spectrum. Ultimately, the MST-CQDs/MIP were kept under drying in a hot air oven for 5 hrs at 60°C. The same procedure was continued for preparing MST- CQDs/NIP (Non-Imprinted Polymer) without the template (CPL) molecule (Scheme 1B).

In a 3 mL cuvette, a dispersed 2.5 mg/mL of MST-CQDs/MIP in 2 mL of phosphate buffer (pH.8) via sonication was added. Afterwards, A well-known concentration of CPL was added. The excitation wavelength was fixed as 330 nm and the fluorescence spectrum was measured the range between 340 and 650 nm. The fluorescence behaviour of proposed sensor was studied in presence and absence of CPL. All the experiments were repeated for more than two times.

10 mL of locally purchased honey was mixed with 4 mL of acetonitrile solvent and allowed to precipitate by stirring for 10 min. After removing the protein, the mixture was ultra sonicated for 30 min. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and further used for analysis. The milk samples were purchased from local market. The pure milk sample must contain lipids and proteins that may cause false results. In order to remove these two, we have treated 20 mL of milk sample with 10 % trichloroacetic acid. The mixture then sonicated for 30 min and the final residue was separated through centrifugation at 3500 rpm. The protein free samples were collected and filtered to acquire a clear solution [30]. The real samples of human urine and blood serum were collected from healthy volunteers in Chang Gung memorial hospital, Taipei, Taiwan. All experiments were performed in accordance with the Ethics Committee Guidelines by the “Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Chang-Gung Memorial Hospital (Approval number – IRB No. 201801660B), Taiwan’’. Experiments were approved by the ethics committee at the Ming Chi University of Technology, New Taipei City, Taiwan. All the experiments conducted in this work followed the ethical standards of the IRB Committee of Chang-Gung Memorial Hospital and proper informed consent was received prior to the studies from those who are participated. Large particles were filtered out before using the collected lake water.

In this work, the fabrication of MST-CQDs/MIP is pointing on the fact that CQDs can act as a fluorophore for the visual readout. On the other hand, the whole MIP structure can form a shell in order to provide a selective cavity for the CPL (template) molecule and therefore effectively avoid the interferences towards the CQDs. The CPL molecule was selectively bind on the MIP cavity through hydrogen bond between amino groups of APTES and hydroxyl groups of CPL. After washing, the molecular recognition site was formed on the MIP surface and thus utilised for the selective detection of CPL molecules even from the real samples. The NH3 solution act as catalyst for the formation of imprinted layer. An extravagant study has been conducted for confirming the formation of CQDs and adsorption of CPL on the vacant spaces of MIPs. Initially, the FT-IR spectrum of purpald and MST has been studied extensively (Fig. S1a, b). Remarkably, the absence of doublet peaks of primary amine at 3400 cm−1 and CO characteristics peak of formaldehyde at 1750 cm−1 confirms the elimination of unwanted reactants from the final MST solution through extended dialysis. Moreover, the S-H stretching vibration was found at 2649 cm−1. The characteristics NN stretching vibration was obtained at 1579 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 A) of MST-CQDs proves the presence of –OH and N-H groups by the formation of stretching vibration peak at 3295 cm−1. The band at 1643 cm−1 pointing towards the typical stretching bands of CN bond. The several peaks at 1088 and 1049 cm−1 indicates the bending vibrations of CO and CS bonds. Thus, all the predicted functional groups are present on the surface of CQDs. After the formation of imprinted polymer through the polymerization by APTES in presence of Ammonia as a catalyst, the broad peak between 1050 and 1100 cm−1 corresponds the presence of Si-O-Si symmetric stretching bond and Si-O-C vibration bonds. The results further established the binding of Silane polymerized MIPs and NIPs on the surface of MST-CQDs. Then, more in-depth FT-IR studies were conducted on MST-CQDs/MIP after the removal of CPL and the NIPs. The presence of broad peaks at 1530 and 3520 cm−1 indicates the existence of N-H stretching band corresponds to the presence of aminopropyl group. The bending vibrational bond at 1410 cm−1 indicates the existence of OH bond on MIPs and NIPs. Moreover, we were studied the FT-IR spectrum of CPL and MST-CQDs/MIP after the addition of CPL. The characteristics peaks of CPL include the strong band at 2881 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of C-H stretching vibrational peaks of CPL and O-H stretching was found at 3450 cm−1. The stretching bond of NO2 was confirmed at 1512 cm−1 on CPL spectrum as well as CPL attached MIPs further confirms the presence of CPL on the MIPs. Also, the stretching bond at 682 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of C-Cl bond on both the structures. Thus, through the deep-rooted studies we were established the formation of imprinted polymer matrix on the surface of MST- CQDs. Also, FT-IR spectra of MIP in presence of CPL also attributes to the expected results and similar peaks were observed on comparison with CPL alone. Thus, it was further confirmed the successful binding of CPL on the surface of MIPs. The FT-IR spectra of NIPs and MIPs after the removal of CPL was discussed and received almost similar results. All these results correspond UV findings on the removal of CPL from MIP matrix by washing.

The XRD (Fig. 1B) patterns of MST −CQDs/MIP and NIPs were subjected to extended studies. The formation of Broad single peak at 2θ = 23.9° shows the presence of amorphous structure with (0 0 2) plane due to polymer matrix and carbonaceous material as CQDs [31]. It is further confirmed by the interlayer spacing value. The d spacing value obtained as 3.5 Å which almost similar to the graphitic structure d-spacing value [32]. All these findings exclusively mention the amorphous nature. The similar XRD patterns confirms the structure analogues of MIPs and NIPs even after the washing process. The d-spacing was found by using Bragg’s equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
n λ = 2 d
sin θ
where, ‘θ’ is the Bragg’s angle with an order ‘n’ and ‘d’ is the lattice spacing. The MST-CQDs XRD pattern exhibits the similar peak at 24° correspond to the amorphous nature, the same pattern was reflected in the case MIP and NIP derived from the CQDs through imprinted polymer technique.

The non imprinted and imprinted polymers after wash were subjected to Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S2). The extraction of ethanol content from the washed polymers is resulted in the initial weight loss of 10 to 13 % at below 100 °C. The residual water content was loss at 140°C for both the MIP and NIP. The degradation of silica polymeric network was similar for MST-CQDs/MIP and NIP. At the same time, the MIP moity remain stable until 280 °C where as that of NIP was degraded even at 200 °C. These differences directing towards the modification taken place during the template removal step for MIP. At last, the polymer structure was completely smashed at 400 °C. All these findings were proved the stability of polymer moity at even higher temperatures [33]. Moreover, these results are acceptable since our applications mostly takes place at room temperature conditions.

To study the adsorption properties of MST-CQDs/MIP, the N2-adsorption and desorption isotherms were performed (Fig. 1C). The structure of both isotherms was almost similar to the type -III hysteresis curve. Thus, the isotherm pointing to the highly porous nature of our sample. The BET surface area of MST-CQDs/MIP was found to be 94 m2/g and the corresponding pore size distribution were calculated using Barrett Joyner Halenda (BJH) technique and the pore size was found to be 1.7 nm along with pore volume as 0.187 cc/g [34].

The chemical compositions of MST-CQDs/MIP were further investigated by XPS analysis. The survey spectrum (Fig. 1D) of MST-CQD/MIP has shown several prominent peaks and all those peaks further verifies the successful doping of Nitrogen and Sulphur on MST-CQDs and strong bonding between the CQDs and MIP matrix. The deconvoluted peak of Si2p (Fig. 1E) has revealed the presence of Si-O-Si at 102.6 eV and further confirms the strong interaction of Si-O and Si-C by the formation of corresponding peak at 101.2 and 99.8 eV. These results further constitute the polymer layer creation on the surface of MST-CQDs. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) has shown four dominant peaks. The peak at 396.4 eV which results the presence of CNC bond which results from the MST-CQDs structure. The presence of N at 397.9 eV corresponds to the interactions occurred at amino group from APTES during the MIP matrix formation. The remaining peaks at 398.8 and 399.7 eV shows the presence of C-N and CNC bonds [35]. More importantly, the C1s spectra (Fig. 1G) of MST-CQDs/MIP, the interaction between C-N and C-S has been confirmed through the peaks at 286.1 and 284.3 eV. Thus, it was further contributing to the previous finding of successful doping of N and S atoms on MST-CQDs. The remaining two peaks are CC and CO bonds which is common in any other CQD [36].

The O1s deconvoluted spectrum (Fig. 1H) has shown two distinct peaks at 529.8 and 530.5 eV which pointing towards the presence of Si-O and CO bonds [37]. Finally, the S2p spectrum (Fig. 1I) has shown three prominent peaks at 164.7, 165.9 and 167.1 eV correspondingly the C-SH, C-S and S-O bonds. All these findings pointing towards the presence of mentioned elements on the polymer. The doping on CQDs by N and S elements were also confirmed by XPS analysis and these factors were further endowing the bright fluorescence with excellent solubility in aqueous media. The overall XPS analysis outputs were highly correlating with the FT-IR results.

In addition to the XRD results, the morphology studies were carried out for MST-CQDs/MIP. Initially, we have characterized the MST – CQDs alone through TEM (Fig. 2 A). The formation of quasi spherical like structure has been further confirmed the formation of CQDs. The well dispersed nature of MST-CQDs is responsible for the bright emission intensity. The particle size was calculated to be 3.4 nm. The lattice distance was found to be 0.23 nm (Fig. 2B), which is matching with the previous reports of CQDs [38].

The TEM images exhibit a well dispersed nature of particles and almost similar in structure pointing towards the precise synthesis method for the MST-CQDs by hydrothermal technique with moderate temperature. The TEM images of MIP were confirming the presence of spherical like structure of CQDs with a polymer layer on the surface (Fig. 2C). The interplanar distance (Fig. 2D) were found and calculated as 0.22 nm, which indicates the presence of graphitic layer on MIP [39]. The single particle images of MIPs (Fig. 2D inset) are clearly confirming the formation of polymer layer on the surface of as prepared MST-CQDs. All these findings correspond to the successful formation of molecular imprinted polymer matrix with specific binding sites. The SEM images (Fig. 2 E&F) of MST- CQDs/MIP has shown the spherical like structure of MIPs. The silica layer with uniform structure was dispersed over the MST-CQDs surface were one of the best ways of recognizing micro emulsion method.

The optical studies were conducted for the MST-CQDs/MIPs, NIPs and MST CQDs distinctly. The in-depth optical studies confirm the binding and fluorescence nature of imprinted polymer. The absorption studies of MST and MST CQDs were confirmed the formation of MST. Purpald having a greater number of nitrogen and a sulphur group, the availability of sufficient number of valence electrons helps to bind with similar structured carbon atoms. Once the purpald reacts with little amount of formaldehyde in alkaline medium on open air turns to an intense purple colour which confirms the structural conversion to MST at room temperature without any loss of N and S atoms [40]. Thus, we have chosen N and S co doped MST as the starting precursor for the preparation of CQDs.

The UV–Vis studies (Fig. 3 A) of starting precursor MST have shown a peak at 235 nm and 535 nm which confirms the conversion from purpald. The same peak was vanished for CQDs derived from the MST. These results confirm the elimination of unreacted MST particles from the CQDs or complete utilization of MST for the synthesis of N, S co-doped CQDs. The MST-CQDs exhibits a slight hump at 275 nm proves the formation of CQDs with n-π* transition of CO [41]. Also, the CQDs derived MIPs has shown a small bend at 254 nm. More importantly, the UV absorption studies aided much significance on the removal of template after the washing process of MST-CQDs/MIP (Fig. 3B). After each washing in ethanol: water mixture and centrifugation, we have studied the absorption spectrum of MIPs. After Centrifugation, the supernatant has been subjected to UV analysis. Interestingly, after the 5th washing, the absorption peak of CPL was vanished and thus confirmed the complete removal of CPL from MIPs. The drastic decrease in the absorption spectrum of CPL has confirmed the efficiency of washing process and complete removal of CPL from imprinted site.

One of the major characteristics of CQDs are their advanced fluorescence activity. In this case the presence of co doping elements such as Nitrogen and Sulphur has been aided much intensity toward the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs. The Fig. 3C, represents the fluorescence spectrum of MST- CQDs, when the sample excited at 330 nm. The emission colour was found to be blue at 426 nm. The resultant brighter blue emission pointing towards the dopant effect of N and S atoms. The different excitation spectrum of MST-CQDs has been revealed (Fig. 3D). The excitation wavelength starting form 300 nm to 330 nm with 10 nm increment, the emission intensity has been increased tremendously and has received maximum at 426 nm upon excitation at 330 nm excitation. Thereby, shows a stepwise reduction in the emission intensity on each increment. Apart from the drastic decrease, the emission wavelength was red shifted towards higher wavelength on each excitation denotes the excitation dependent behaviour of CQDs like most of the other CQDs.

In order to confirm the recognition competence of MIP, we have studied the quenching ability of CPL on MST – CQDs alone (Fig. 3E). As expected, there were no noticeable changes observed with the emission intensity of CQDs in presence of CPL even at higher concentration of CPL. Therefore, the quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed due to the binding of CPL with recognition cavity available on the MIP matrix but not due to the CQDs. Finally, as portrayed in Fig. 3F, the fluorescence intensity of MST-CQDs/MIP was quenched tremendously after the addition of CPL with various concentration but the proper removal (washing with water and ethanol mixture) of CPL molecule from the binding cavity of MIP, the 95 % of fluorescence activity was restored like MST-CQDs/NIP. Thus, MST-CQDs/MIP exhibits bright blue fluorescence in the absence of CPL and colourless after the addition of CPL under UV light (Fig. 3F Inset). All of our findings correspond to the greater feasibility of MIP towards the binding and recombine of CPL under optimum reaction condition.

For achieving the wider applications of MST-CQDs/MIP, it is inevitable to find the optimum pH level (Fig. S3a). We have chosen phosphate buffer solution for confirming the stable pH of MST-CQDs/MIP. We have been studied the various pH ranging from pH 4 to pH 11. The emission intensity of MIPs has shown linear rise from pH 4 to pH 7 and has received maximum intensity at pH 8 and from pH 8 to pH 11 further decrease in the intensity was noticed. Thus, we have chosen pH 8 as the optimum and stable pH for the MST-CQDs/MIP for further studies. The reverse micro emulsion method for the synthesis of MIPs in presence of Silica and Ammonia may leads to the shifting of pH to 8. The ionic strength of MST-CQDs/MIP was investigated in presence of various concentration of NaCl ranging from 50 mM to 300 mM of concentration (Fig. S3b). As expected, the MST-CQDs/MIP shown a excellent tolerating level towards the high concentration of NaCl. The sensor system was shown stable emission up to 250 mM concentration of NaCl and afterwards has exhibits slight decrease in the intensity. Thus, as a concluding part, the MIPs was stable even in the presence of 250 mM concentration of NaCl.

In order to evaluate sensing protocol and application, it is crucial to study the storage stability of the probe. The fabricated MST-CQDs/MIP was exposed to stability studies (Fig. S4). The freshly prepared sample emission intensity was compared with the aged samples ranging from one day gap to two-month time. There are no notable changes found in the case of two-month-old sample when compared to freshly prepared sample. Thus, it is confirmed that our sensor system remains stable for more than two months. Further, we have studied the fluorescence response time of MST-CQDs/MIP towards the detection of CPL molecule. The fluorescence quenching in the presence of 3.72 μM CPL was monitored in MST-CQDs/MIP working solution. The decrease in fluorescence intensity was monitored immediately after the addition of CPL. The quenching was stabilized after 3 min of addition of CPL (Fig. S5). We have monitored the fluorescence response from 0 min to 10 min in presence of CPL. After 3 min, the response was stabilized and unaltered further. Thus, we have optimized the response time as 3 min for MST-CQDs/MIP sensor.

The recognition ability of MIPs was extensively studied in presence of CPL (Fig. 4 A). For better understanding regarding the sensing properties, the fluorescence spectra of MST-CQDs/MIP and MST-CQDs/NIP was studied broadly in presence of CPL. A series of various concentration of CPL was studied on both the systems. The MST-CQDs/MIP and NIP shows fluorescence quenching in presence of CPL. Even though both sensor system shows the fluorescence quenching on addition of various concentration of CPL, the quenching proportion of MST-CQDs/MIP is way better than NIP. These significant difference among the MIP and NIP corresponds to the recognition ability of MIP. The relationship between fluorescence quenching ratio of MIP and NIP on addition of various concentration of CPL as analyte was further studied by Stern Volmer equation as below (Eq. (2)),
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q

where, “F0” and “F” are the fluorescence intensity of MST-CQDs/MIP and NIP before and after the addition of CPL, “Q” is the concentration of CPL on each addition and “Ksv” is the quenching constant. The binding ability of both MST – CQDs/MIP and MST – CQDs/NIP towards CPL was found by ratio of Ksv of MIP on Ksv of NIP.

Generally, the ratio of Ksv(MST-CQDs/MIP) and Ksv(MST-CQDs/NIP) was commonly termed as Imprinting factor (IF). Usually, the higher binding ability of MIPs towards the analyte compared to NIPs makes greater IF values. In our case, the IF value was found to be 2.34 which corresponds to the superior binding capacity of MST-CQDs/MIP on the approach of CPL.

The linear correlation graph (Fig. 4B) was plotted for MST-CQDs/MIP and the sensor system was covered the linear range between 0.24 μM and 26.1 μM with steady decrease in the emission intensity. Apart from this, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 7.65 nM by means of standard formula (Eq. (3)) [42];
(3)
LOD =
3 σ / m
(where “m” corresponds to the slope of linear calibration plot and “σ” is the standard deviation of blank current response).

On the other hand, MST-CQDs/NIP with lack of proper binding site on the imprinting matrix leads to the lower affinity towards CPL (Fig. 4C). The MST-CQDs/NIP shows poor response towards CPL concentration and corresponding linear range was covered only short range of CPL concentration (Fig. 4D). All these findings directing towards the high affinity of CPL towards the MIP imprinting matrix with outstanding recognition capability.

Further, we have studied the CPL reported literatures extensively and found our system is more compatible and highly competitive towards the detection of CPL molecule (Table 1 ). We have carried out a detailed comparison with previously reported literatures including different techniques such as optical, electrochemical sensor works and found our proposed sensor system has shown higher competence including in terms of LOD, Linear range, applicability and exhibits good recovery range in real samples. Moreover, we have performed the recyclability analysis for the MST-CQDs/MIPs with proper binding and removal cycles.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CPL greatly overlaps with the excitation spectra of CQDs/MIP which results in the quenching of fluorescence emission. Thus, these findings further open up the possibilities of Inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. 4E). Mostly, CPL possess higher molar extinction coefficient at 290 nm which plays a key role in the stronger IFE nature of MST-CQDs/MIP. A greater Molar extinction coefficient of CPL is associated with a higher spectral overlap region with the probe and resulted in a greater fluorescence quenching. Thus, the sensitivity of the CPL detection as the variation in absorbance of the CPL can be translated into an exponential change in the MST-CQDs/MIP emission signals [53]. Here we have calculated the molar extinction coefficient or Molar absorptivity (ε) of CPL via spectrophotometer with the help of Beer Lambert’s law (A = εcl) at absorption (A = 290 nm) and concentration (c) was set to be 1.5 μg/ml and path length (l) 1 cm. Thus, molar absorptivity was found to be 2.4 × 104 M−1 cm−1. Thus, an effective overlap would be possible at the excitation wavelength of the fluorophore. Moreover, the UV absorption spectrum of MST- CQDs/MIP was obtained at 280 nm. The spectrum was almost similar to the CPL absorption spectrum. The peak was found nearly equivalent to 290 nm (Fig. 4F). Due to this similarity in UV–Vis absorption spectrum, when electron excited from the valence band of CQDs to the higher state, the tendency to jump the excited electron from the conduction band of CQDs to the lowest unoccupied orbital of CPL leads to a non-radiative relaxation phenomena [54]. All these results further aid the quenching of MIPs fluorescence emission.

The formation of complex (Meisenheimer) between the CPL molecule and amino group of APTES leads to the energy transfer from the excited electron of MST-CQDs towards the complex and finally resulted in drastic quenching in fluorescence (Fig. 4G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The regeneration studies of proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The recyclability of proposed sensor probe was investigated thoroughly as follows (Fig S6), the 2.5 mg/mL of MST-CQDs/MIP embedded with 26.1 µM of CPL was prepared and corresponding fluorescence activity was recorded after 60s. After reading, the CPL molecule was eluted via proper elution process by using methanol and acetic acid mixture as discussed in section 2.5 and followed by centrifugation at 4500 rpm. The MST-CQDs/MIP was treated again with CPL molecule under the same condition like before. The same procedure was repeated for 5 times. As expected, the emission intensity has shown drastic decrease in presence of CPL and retain the emission capacity after elution of CPL. We have carried out the regeneration process for 5 times. The emission intensity was retained almost 95 % as of first cycle.

The fluorescence quenching capability of MST-CQDs/MIP in presence of CPL was further utilized for the selectivity studies in presence of potential interference molecules (Fig. 4H). Here, we have chosen similar antibiotics and other biomolecules for selectivity studies. K+, Na+ cations also subjected for the studies. The concentration of each interference molecule was fixed as 0.5 M and that of CPL was fixed at 4.95 μM, which is much lower than other interreferences concentration. The fluorescence response of MST-CQDs/MIP was confirmed the high affinity towards the CPL molecule.

Interestingly, the higher concentration of any of these interferences, does not involve in the selective detection capability of our proposed sensor towards the lower concentration of CPL. All these fascinating results pointing towards the high affinity of MST-CQDs/MIP towards the target even in the presence of high concentration of other interfering molecules.

The practical applicability of MST-CQDs/MIP was further studied in presence of various real samples including honey, milk, human blood serum, urine and tap water (Table 2 ). Initially, none of these samples contain any traces of CPL. Thus, we have spiked a known concentration of CPL to each sample and analysed in presence of MST-CQDs/MIP by using spectrofluorometer. Interestingly, our system has shown excellent recovery of CPL from these real samples. The recovery ranges from 97 % to 103 % which shows the high detection capability of our sensor system towards the CPL. All these findings suggested the high selectivity, sensitivity and even very high accuracy of MST-CQDs/MIP for the detection of CPL.

In this context, we are reporting the fluorescent sensor based on CQDs grafted on Molecular imprinted polymer (MST-CQDs/MIP) for CPL sensor. We have followed reverse micro emulsion method for the preparation of MIPs. Our sensor system has shown great advantages over the other analytes due to the specific recognition ability on template (CPL) molecule. The morphological studies confirmed the polymeric layer with spherical structure. The proposed sensor system has shown excellent selectivity among the other drug molecules and analytes. The detection capability of proposed sensor was studied on comparison with non-imprinted system. The LOD value was calculated to be 7.65 nM from linear plot with various concentration of CPL likely from 0.24 to 26.1 μM. Further, we have done the recyclability test for the MIP through five cycles. Finally, we have applied the MST-CQDs/MIP for the detection of CPL from the real samples including clinical and food samples.
